expertReview.title: Senior Motoring Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: A well-rounded machine that delivers on the brochure promise. The engineering is honest, the cabin is built to last and the driving experience makes a strong case for itself in the segment.

cons: Firm low-speed ride on rough roads

reviews.name: Rohit S.
reviews.title: Worth every rupee
reviews.body: Owned it for six months and it still feels like a new car every morning. The build quality is fantastic and the engine never feels strained.

reviews.name: Ananya K.
reviews.title: Daily driver dream
reviews.body: Comfortable on long highways, surprisingly easy in traffic. The infotainment took some getting used to but it grows on you.

reviews.name: Vikram T.
reviews.title: Pure driver's car
reviews.body: Steering feel is sublime, brakes are very progressive. Service costs are on the higher side but expected at this price point.

faqs.q: What is the on-road price?
faqs.a: On-road price varies by city and includes road tax, insurance and registration. The numbers shown here are indicative for Delhi and are refreshed periodically.

faqs.q: Is there a waiting period?
faqs.a: Waiting periods depend on the variant and city, and typically range from 4 to 12 weeks. Dealers can confirm exact timelines on enquiry.

faqs.q: What is the service interval?
faqs.a: The manufacturer recommends service every 10,000 km or 12 months, whichever comes first. Use only authorised service centres to preserve the warranty.

faqs.q: How is the warranty structured?
faqs.a: A standard 2-year unlimited-kilometre warranty applies, extendable up to 5 years. Battery components on electric variants are covered separately for 8 years.

faqs.q: Can I customise the colour after booking?
faqs.a: Yes — the configurator on this page mirrors what is bookable today. Colour swaps are possible up to the point of allocation, after which changes may incur a fee.

maruti-suzuki-alto-k10.tagline: India's favourite city hatch. Smarter K10.
maruti-suzuki-alto-k10.about: The Alto K10 is built for tight city streets and tight budgets. A peppy 1.0L K10C engine, light kerb weight and Maruti's service footprint make it the default first car for millions.

expertReview.title: Senior Motoring Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: Not about drama — about doing the basics brilliantly for the money. Light steering and low running costs make it the rational first-car pick.

reviews.name: Rahul P.
reviews.title: Does what it promises
reviews.body: Happy with the Alto K10 after daily use. Running costs and service experience match expectations for the segment.

reviews.name: Sneha K.
reviews.title: Solid daily choice
reviews.body: Comfortable for commutes, easy to park. Would like a bit more boot space but overall very satisfied.

reviews.name: Arjun M.
reviews.title: Worth the money
reviews.body: Feature list and safety for the price impressed us. Dealer experience was smooth.

faqs.q: What is the on-road price of Alto K10?
faqs.a: Ex-showroom starts around ₹3.70 Lakh. On-road prices typically start around ₹4.45 Lakh; top petrol AMT and CNG variants cost more depending on city, insurance and accessories.

faqs.q: Is there a waiting period?
faqs.a: Popular colours and top trims may have a 2–8 week wait depending on city. Your dealer can confirm allocation timelines.

faqs.q: Petrol, diesel or CNG — which should I pick?
faqs.a: This model is primarily offered with Petrol. Choose based on your monthly running — city commuters often prefer petrol or CNG; highway users may prefer diesel where available.

faqs.q: How is the service network?
faqs.a: Maruti Suzuki has authorised service centres in most Indian metros and tier-2 cities. Stick to scheduled service to keep warranty intact.

faqs.q: Is it good for first-time buyers?
faqs.a: If your budget and use-case match this segment, it is a sensible choice. Test drive both manual and automatic trims if offered.

maruti-suzuki-wagonr.about: WagonR's boxy silhouette isn't just styling — it translates to headroom, visibility and a surprisingly usable cabin for families on a budget. Available with petrol and S-CNG, it's the practical Maruti many grow up with.

faqs.q: Petrol, diesel or CNG — which should I pick?
faqs.a: This model is primarily offered with Petrol. Choose based on your monthly running — city commuters often prefer petrol or CNG; highway users may prefer diesel where available.

faqs.q: How is the service network?
faqs.a: Land Rover has authorised service centres in most Indian metros and tier-2 cities. Stick to scheduled service to keep warranty intact.

faqs.q: Is it good for first-time buyers?
faqs.a: If your budget and use-case match this segment, it is a sensible choice. Test drive both manual and automatic trims if offered.

ferrari-roma.about: Roma is Ferrari's elegant front-engined GT — 3.9L twin-turbo V8, gorgeous proportions and a more relaxed character than mid-engine models. Grand touring in the truest sense.

expertReview.title: Senior Motoring Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: Beauty and speed in equal measure. The Ferrari for buyers who want grace as much as pace.

reviews.name: Rahul P.
reviews.title: Does what it promises
reviews.body: Happy with the Roma after daily use. Running costs and service experience match expectations for the segment.

reviews.name: Sneha K.
reviews.title: Solid daily choice
reviews.body: Comfortable for commutes, easy to park. Would like a bit more boot space but overall very satisfied.

reviews.name: Arjun M.
reviews.title: Worth the money
reviews.body: Feature list and safety for the price impressed us. Dealer experience was smooth.

faqs.q: What is the on-road price of Roma?
faqs.a: Ex-showroom starts around ₹3.56 Cr. On-road pricing typically starts around ₹4.09 Cr; higher variants cost more depending on city, registration, insurance and accessories.

faqs.q: Is there a waiting period?
faqs.a: Popular colours and top trims may have a 2–8 week wait depending on city. Your dealer can confirm allocation timelines.

faqs.q: Petrol, diesel or CNG — which should I pick?
faqs.a: This model is primarily offered with Petrol. Choose based on your monthly running — city commuters often prefer petrol or CNG; highway users may prefer diesel where available.

faqs.q: How is the service network?
faqs.a: Ferrari has authorised service centres in most Indian metros and tier-2 cities. Stick to scheduled service to keep warranty intact.

faqs.q: Is it good for first-time buyers?
faqs.a: If your budget and use-case match this segment, it is a sensible choice. Test drive both manual and automatic trims if offered.

hero.headline:
Find the perfect bike. 
 Built for your ride.
hero.description: From city commuters to track-ready beasts.

detailDefaults.tagline: Built to ride. Refined to thrill.
detailDefaults.about: A motorcycle obsessed with the details — geometry tuned for confidence, electronics tuned for fun, and finish that lasts the long road. Equally happy on a Sunday twisty as on a daily commute.
detailDefaults.priceNote: Ex-showroom and on-road prices shown for the city of Delhi. Final pricing varies by city, dealer and accessories.

expertReview.title: Two-Wheel Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: A well-rounded motorcycle that delivers what the brochure promises. Strong fundamentals, predictable handling and a cabin of buttons that actually make sense — there's not much to complain about here.

cons: Heat on the legs in traffic

reviews.name: Aarav P.
reviews.title: Daily smiles
reviews.body: Sharp throttle response, predictable brakes — exactly what I wanted from a daily motorcycle. Service interval is generous too.

reviews.name: Sneha R.
reviews.title: Composed long-haul
reviews.body: Did a 600 km weekend ride and the ergonomics are spot on. Wind protection at the screen could be better but otherwise brilliant.

reviews.name: Devansh M.
reviews.title: Built to last
reviews.body: Two seasons in and not a single rattle. Paint and chrome still look new. Worth every rupee.

faqs.q: What is the on-road price?
faqs.a: On-road price includes road tax, insurance and registration. The figure shown here is indicative for Delhi and is refreshed periodically.

faqs.q: Is there a waiting period?
faqs.a: Waiting periods vary by colour and city — usually 2 to 8 weeks. Your dealer can confirm exact timelines on enquiry.

faqs.q: What is the service interval?
faqs.a: The manufacturer recommends service every 6,000 km or 6 months, whichever comes first. Use only authorised service centres to preserve the warranty.

faqs.q: How is the warranty structured?
faqs.a: A standard 2-year unlimited-kilometre warranty applies, extendable up to 5 years. EV components on electric variants are covered separately for 3 years.

faqs.q: Are accessories included in the price?
faqs.a: No. Crash protection, panniers, alternate seats and quickshifter kits are dealer-fitted options that add to the on-road price.

bmw-s1000rr.about: The S 1000 RR has spent over a decade rewriting what a road-legal superbike can do. A 999cc inline-four that revs to the moon, ShiftCam variable valve timing for sane road manners, and an electronics suite that makes you look like a hero on track. It's brutal when you ask it to be — and surprisingly polite when you don't.

expertReview.title: Track Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: The S 1000 RR has gone from the angriest litre-bike on the block to the most usable, without losing any of its bite. The chassis, electronics and brakes are class-leading; the engine is sublime. If you can ride it well, very few road bikes will keep up.

cons: Hot on the inner thighs in traffic
Aggressive ergonomics for long highway rides
Service costs add up at full intervals

reviews.name: Rajat S.
reviews.title: Pure rocket
reviews.body: Every gear pull feels physically improbable. Brakes are massive, the up-and-down quickshifter is flawless. Best superbike I've owned.

reviews.name: Nikhil P.
reviews.title: Daily superbike
reviews.body: Surprised by how usable it is in traffic with the rider modes. Once you find an open road, it's a different animal.

reviews.name: Aastha G.
reviews.title: Heat is real
reviews.body: Performance is otherworldly, but be prepared for engine heat on slow days. Service interval and parts are pricey.

faqs.q: Is the S 1000 RR comfortable for daily use?
faqs.a: More than you'd expect for a litre-class superbike, thanks to rider modes and ShiftCam VVT. That said, the ergonomics are sport-biased and not ideal for hour-long traffic crawls every day.

faqs.q: Do I need an M Sport for the track?
faqs.a: No — the Standard and Pro are more than enough for amateur track use. M Sport adds carbon wheels and an M Sport livery, which are bragging rights more than capability.

faqs.q: Is a quickshifter standard?
faqs.a: Yes — Pro Shift Assistant (up and down) is standard across all India trims, alongside launch control and a comprehensive electronics package.

faqs.q: What's the service cost like?
faqs.a: Expect annual service of roughly ₹25,000 – ₹40,000 depending on usage. Tyres and brake pads are the biggest consumables.

faqs.q: Is there a fuel grade requirement?
faqs.a: BMW recommends 95+ RON. In a pinch 91 RON works but the ignition map de-tunes slightly to preserve the engine.

ktm-390-duke.about: The 390 Duke is the bike that taught a generation of Indian riders what a real performance machine feels like. A peppy 399cc single, sharp steel-trellis chassis, and styling that still polarises a decade in. Lightweight, brutally quick for its capacity, and built for hooliganism on every commute.

expertReview.title: Naked Bike Editor, RideBuilders
expertReview.verdict: Every time KTM updates the 390 Duke they make it sharper, lighter on the wrists and easier to live with. The 2024 version finally has the refinement to match the manic chassis — it's the most complete sub-₹4 L performance bike on sale, full stop.

reviews.name: Ishaan T.
reviews.title: Pocket rocket
reviews.body: Light, quick, sounds great. The quickshifter is genuinely useful even in traffic. Mileage holds up around 22-24 even with spirited riding.

reviews.name: Priya D.
reviews.title: Brilliant for new riders
reviews.body: Coming from a 200cc this feels like a leap, but in a manageable way. Vibrations above 110 km/h are the only real annoyance.

faqs.q: Is the 390 Duke a good beginner bike?
faqs.a: It's better as a second bike. The power is approachable but the chassis and brakes are aggressive — a rider with even a year of experience will get more out of it.

faqs.q: What's the real-world mileage?
faqs.a: 21–25 km/l in mixed riding. Hard riding drops it closer to 18 km/l; highway cruising lifts it to 28 km/l.

faqs.q: Is the quickshifter included in the base trim?
faqs.a: Yes — both up and down quickshifter, ride modes, cornering ABS and the 5" TFT are standard across all India variants.

faqs.q: How does it compare to the 390 Adventure?
faqs.a: The Adventure shares the engine but adds wire-spoke wheels, longer-travel suspension and a more upright riding triangle. The Duke is sharper on tarmac; the Adventure is more comfortable everywhere else.

faqs.q: What's the seat height like?
faqs.a: 820 mm — taller than most commuter bikes. Riders below 5'5" may need to consider a low-seat option or platform shoes.

kawasaki-z900.about: Kawasaki's Z900 has always been the inline-four naked at a price that undercut the German and Italian competition by enough to make people stop and think. Smooth, fast, surprisingly easy to live with, and an exhaust note that genuinely raises the hair on your neck.

ducati-panigale-v4.about: The Panigale V4 is the closest you can get to MotoGP without a paddock pass. A 1103cc V4 lifted straight from the Desmosedici programme, aerodynamic winglets that actually do something, and electronics that make the whole package usable. There is nothing else like it.

bmw-gs-1250.about: The GS is the bike most adventure riders measure every other ADV against. A 1254cc Boxer twin with characterful, useable torque from low revs, telelever suspension that swallows broken tarmac whole, and ergonomics that genuinely work for all-day rides. Heavy, but transcends its weight the moment it's moving.

hero.description: Quick answers to the things readers ask us most. Can't find what you need? Drop us a line — we read everything.

items.q: How do I decide between two vehicles I'm interested in?
items.a: Open both on the comparison page — we'll show specs, prices and reviews side by side, with the best value in each row highlighted. You can also save your top picks for later.
Ex-showroom prices are refreshed weekly from manufacturer announcements. On-road prices are estimated for the city of Delhi unless you select another city. Final dealer quotes can vary.

items.q: Do you sell cars or bikes?
items.a: No. RideBuilders is a comparison and information platform. We never sell vehicles and we don't take a commission on any purchase.

items.q: Can you connect me to a dealer?
items.a: You can request a dealer callback directly from any vehicle detail page. We pass your contact details to the nearest authorised dealer — that's it.

items.q: How many vehicles can I compare at once?
items.a: Up to four vehicles side by side on desktop, and two on mobile. You can swap any slot at any time.

items.q: Are bike vs car comparisons supported?
items.a: Yes, but only on common dimensions like price, top speed, mileage and zero-to-hundred. Vehicle-class-specific specs (boot space, lean angle) are hidden when comparing across categories.
From manufacturer brochures, government type-approval filings and verified owner reports. Every spec has a source which you can hover over to see.

items.q: Do I need an account to use RideBuilders?
items.a: No. Browsing, comparing and reading reviews are all available without signing up. An account lets you save shortlists and get price-drop alerts.

items.q: How do I delete my account?
items.a: Email us at hello@ridebuilders.in from your registered email and we'll wipe your account and all associated data within 7 days.
The bare minimum — page-view analytics (anonymised) and the data you give us when you create an account or request a dealer callback. See our Privacy page for the full breakdown.

items.q: Do you share my information with dealers?
items.a: Only the contact details you explicitly submit on a dealer-callback form, and only with the dealer you chose. We never sell user lists.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: How we collect, use and protect your data — written without the lawyer speak.

sections.heading: What we collect
sections.body: We collect the minimum data needed to operate RideBuilders. That includes anonymised page-view analytics (which pages are popular, which devices visit), and any information you choose to share with us — name, email, phone — when you create an account or request a dealer callback.

sections.heading: Why we collect it
sections.body: Analytics help us understand which features matter and what's broken. The contact information you submit is used to fulfil the action you requested (e.g. routing a dealer callback) and, with your consent, occasionally to share editorial updates.

sections.heading: Who we share it with
sections.body: We share contact details only with the specific dealer you choose to engage. We do not sell user lists, ever. Our analytics partner (privacy-first, EU-hosted) only receives aggregated, de-identified pageview data.

sections.heading: How long we keep it
sections.body: Account data lives as long as your account does. Analytics data is automatically anonymised after 90 days and aggregated after 12 months. Dealer-callback records are retained for 18 months to help you follow up if needed.

sections.heading: Your rights
sections.body: You can ask for a copy of everything we hold on you, ask us to correct it, or ask us to delete it — all by emailing hello@ridebuilders.in from the email tied to your account. We respond within seven business days.

sections.heading: Cookies
sections.body: We use a small number of strictly-necessary cookies and one analytics cookie. You can manage these from the cookie banner. See our Cookies page for the full list.

sections.heading: Contacting us
sections.body: Privacy questions go to privacy@ridebuilders.in. We aim to respond within two business days. For formal complaints under Indian data-protection law, you can also reach out to the Data Protection Officer at the same address.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.subtitle: The ground rules for using RideBuilders. Short and plain-spoken.

sections.heading: Who we are
sections.body: RideBuilders is an editorial and comparison platform owned and operated by RideBuilders Technologies Pvt. Ltd., a company registered in India. These terms govern your use of ridebuilders.in and any of its sub-domains.

sections.heading: Acceptable use
sections.body: Use the site to research, compare and read about vehicles. Don't scrape it at scale without written permission, don't try to break it, and don't reupload large chunks of our editorial content without crediting and linking back.

sections.heading: Accuracy
sections.body: We work hard to verify every spec and price, but we cannot guarantee absolute accuracy. Treat prices, mileage and feature lists as research starting points, not final dealer quotes. Always confirm with an authorised dealer before purchase.

sections.heading: Reviews and opinions
sections.body: Reviews and opinions are those of the credited authors. They reflect honest assessments based on real testing. Manufacturers occasionally loan us vehicles for review purposes; we disclose this whenever it applies.

sections.heading: User-submitted content
sections.body: If you post a review or rating, you retain copyright but grant us a worldwide, royalty-free licence to host and display it. We may moderate or remove content that breaks our community guidelines.

sections.heading: Limitation of liability
sections.body: RideBuilders is provided on an as-is basis. We're not liable for purchase decisions made on the back of information published here, or for downtime or data loss. Our maximum aggregate liability is limited to the fees you've paid us in the previous twelve months, which is currently zero.

sections.heading: Governing law
sections.body: These terms are governed by the laws of India. Any disputes are subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Gurgaon, Haryana.

cookies.title: Cookies Policy
cookies.subtitle: What cookies we use, what they do and how to turn them off.

sections.heading: Strictly necessary
sections.body: These keep you logged in, remember your dark-mode preference and make features like the comparison shortlist work. They cannot be switched off without breaking the site.

sections.heading: Analytics
sections.body: We use a single privacy-first analytics provider that does not set tracking cookies across other sites. The cookie we set is short-lived and stores only an anonymous session identifier.

sections.heading: Marketing
sections.body: We do not use marketing or advertising cookies. We do not run ads on the site, and we do not embed third-party tracking pixels for retargeting.

sections.heading: Managing cookies
sections.body: You can clear cookies from your browser at any time using the browser's privacy settings. Doing so will log you out and reset your saved comparison shortlists.

hero.headline: The numbers, decoded.
hero.description: Every spec on RideBuilders is verified at source and refreshed weekly. Use the leaderboards below to find the quickest, most powerful or best-value machines — and the glossary to understand what each number actually means.

metrics.label: Most powerful
metrics.description: Peak engine power in horsepower. More headroom up the rev range.

metrics.label: Quickest 0–100
metrics.description: Seconds from a standstill to 100 km/h. Lower is better.

metrics.label: Highest top speed
metrics.description: Manufacturer-claimed maximum velocity, limited or otherwise.

metrics.label: Best value
metrics.description: Lowest ex-showroom price across the catalogue.

glossary.definition: The combustion (or electric) unit at the heart of the vehicle. Quoted as displacement (e.g. 3.0L), cylinder layout (Inline-6) and aspiration (Twin-Turbo).
Peak horsepower the engine produces, usually at a specific RPM. More power means more top-end pace, but real-world feel is shaped by the torque curve and gearing.
The rotational pulling force at the crankshaft, measured in Newton-metres. High torque available low in the rev range = effortless rolling acceleration.
How quickly the vehicle gets from a complete stop to 100 km/h. The most quoted real-world performance number, although launch conditions and traction play a big role.
The vehicle's maximum claimed velocity. Many performance cars are electronically limited (typically to 250 km/h) unless an optional package removes the limit.
Fuel efficiency — quoted as km/l for petrol/diesel and km per charge for EVs. Real-world figures usually trail manufacturer claims by 10 – 30%.
What powers the vehicle — Petrol, Diesel, Hybrid (combustion + electric) or Electric. Each has different running-cost and infrastructure implications.
How power is delivered to the wheels — Manual, Automatic, DCT (dual-clutch) or CVT. Affects driving feel as much as outright performance.
Which wheels are driven — RWD (rear), FWD (front), AWD (all). Drivetrain shapes handling character more than most buyers realise.
